items.title: [正面] It's an amazing gacha take on roguelike-deckbuilde...
items.summary:
It's an amazing gacha take on roguelike-deckbuilders; 
Yet, as people mentioned it earlier, the entire plotline is ChatGPT-translated. 
It is relly hard to keep up with the lore of any given subject: characters often get misgendered (or even mispronounced), emotional moments become confusing since the text doesn't really relay good on what's going on on screen, some of dialogues between characters are unreletated jumble or words that don't lead anywhere. 
 
Still, gameplay-wise, art-wise, music-wise, and especially design-wise, this game is a HUUUGE recommend!

items.title: [正面] Insanely good game. Very under rated. Slay the spi...
items.summary: Insanely good game. Very under rated. Slay the spire meets gotya gaming.

items.title: [正面] Sometimes in life, we make the right decisions for...
items.summary:
Sometimes in life, we make the right decisions for the wrong reasons. I started playing this game to own the weird puriteen who wrote a review crying about a bunch of nonsense that had nothing to do with the game, but I kept playing because I found myself actually liking it.

THE GOOD:
- Excellent character designs. Good character design revolves around telling the viewer about the character without saying a word, and the artist nails it. Ignore the weird puriteens complaining about Thais; they also pretend Concord was good.
- Excellent character [i]writing.[/i] This isn't immediately obvious in the main storyline, but the characters' Psyche Deepdives provide insight into who they are, why they are that way, and how they became Awakeners. Many of the characters have hidden depths, and I defy you to play through Ogier, Erica, or Aurita's story without getting a little choked-up. Even more archetypal characters, like the mad artist Pickman, the cartoonish dominatrix Pandia (though she doesn't have a Deepdive), or the blood-crazed gladiator Uvhash, can subvert expectations in satisfying ways.
- Complex and challenging gameplay. Being averse to RNG-heavy games and "roguelites," I've never played Slay the Spire, but Morimens actually has me wanting to give it a try. Bosses seldom feel unfair or unbalanced, and while the four Realms suggest four gameplay styles, your team composition allows for greater variety than that. You could run a Counter-focused team that stonewalls any incoming attacks; a nigh-immortal rushdown team of blood-crazed baby-eaters; a highly toxic Poison-focused team led by Definitely Not Harley Quinn; a time-warping team starring a street urchin, a magician, a robot, and an insomniac; a tentacle machine-gun menace headed by the cutest and most precious little girl you've ever laid eyes on...there are options within options.
- All the standard Awakeners have their uses and strengths, and even the starter units are perfectly competent if you (ahem) play your cards right. Limited-time Awakeners are powerful, sure, but they're never [i]required.[/i] If any gacha games can truly be called F2P-friendly, this is one of them.
- Fairly generous pulls; 3% rate of pulling an Awakener, guaranteed SR-or-better item every 30 pulls, and on the standard banner, you only pull from the Realm of your choosing, ensuring you're fairly likely to get what you want. The game also gives out Pure Cores like candy while going through the story, and while they can only be used for pulling from the standard banners, see above. (It also gives out Silver like candy, so you can just buy Luminous Cores with it when you want the limited-time Awakeners.)
- Try before you buy! To let you get a feel for their playstyle and mechanics, the game lets you demo any Awakener, even limited-time ones not currently available, at lv50 with decent secondary upgrades, and rewards you with either Pure Cores (for standard Awakeners) or Radix Sediment (for limited-time Awakeners) upon clearing the trial. (You also get a free Luminous Core and stamina-restore item for clearing a limited-time Awakener's trial while their banner's up.)
- Traphase is a pretty good PvP mode. I'm not a huge fan of PvP as a concept, so this is high praise. It's balanced separately from the main game modes, and neither your player level nor your characters' upgrades matter for it, so you aren't automatically screwed out of winning as a new player. It has its problems, and serious play is more about keeping a spreadsheet of when people with certain comps play each day so you can snipe them than about actually having a good comp of your own, but it's a fun way to kill time waiting for your stamina to recharge and/or get your daily out of the way.
- The main storyline, Awakener trials, Lightless Realm, Phantasmal Dive, and D-Tide modes don't cost stamina to play through, although their level requirements are [i]not[/i] suggestions. (You [i]will[/i] have a bad time trying to clear things underleveled.)
- Unexpected anti-frustration features! You can tick a box to disable warning popups for the rest of the day, such as when you try to go into an encounter underleveled, or enter a side-story before you've cleared the relevant content. Surplus XP isn't lost when hitting an Awakener's current level cap; it's saved, and when you've accumulated enough, the extra gets refunded in the form of an XP item. Skipping previously three-starred grindfarming fights awards the same amount of resources and currency as playing through them again would, [i]and[/i] at no extra stamina cost. Stamina [i]will[/i] continue to increase over time after hitting the 300-point cap, just slower.
- It runs great on Linux, with minimal tinkering required and very few graphical errors.

THE NEUTRAL:
- The music and story start off OK; they start getting good around Chapter 2, which can take a while for new players to reach.
- The first arc of the story is now fully human-translated, at least in English, but the second arc, some side-stories, and various other snippets are still machine-translated. The jank is real.
- There are many different currencies and resources to manage. You get used to it eventually.

THE BAD:
- [b][i]Very[/i][/b] aggressive monetization. This game is not [i]nearly[/i] as difficult as it wants you to think it is. The most important factors are your player level and your team's average level, both of which are increased primarily by grinding, not by spending money.
- As mentioned, level requirements are fairly non-negotiable, becoming [i]entirely[/i] non-negotiable around Chapter 5 of the storyline or the lv45 tier of Phantasmal Dive. You'll also need to keep your characters' skills upgraded, which requires its own set of resources.
- Every form of upgrade—whether you're leveling a character, combining lower-tier resources into higher-tier ones, or stacking Wheels of Destiny together—costs Rose Scrips, ie. money, and while money can be grindfarmed like resources, that also means you're spending stamina grindfarming money [i]instead of[/i] resources.

THE UGLY:
- Although the art is good, the colors are scuffed. The UI has serious color banding, even on mobile. Combined with the slightly-desaturated palette, this makes the game look kind of greasy, as if you're looking at it through very thin oilpaper. I don't know how this happened, or if it was intentional, but I recommend using ReShade.
- The anti-aliasing doesn't work too well.

CONCLUSION:
- There is no Thais sexdoll. There isn't even porn of this game, which technically means it doesn't exist. Yet here I am, playing and enjoying it.
